Defendant The Regents Of The University Of California Dba University Of California San Francisco Medical Center'S Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Jul-09-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant The Regents Of The University Of California Dba University Of California San Francisco Medical Center's Motion For Summary Judgment (Per Agreement Of Parties)

Defendant The Regents of the University of California's (d.b.a. University of California San Francisco Medical Center) motion for summary judgment is denied. The record contains conflicting expert opinions regarding the applicable standard of care and whether defendants' treatment fell below that standard of care. (See Munro v. Regents of University of California (1989) 215 Cal.App.3d 977, 983-984 [the standard of care against which the acts of a physician are to be measured is a matter peculiarly within the knowledge of experts; when a defendant provides expert declarations that his conduct fell within the community standard of care, he is entitled to summary judgment unless the plaintiff provides conflicting expert evidence].) Here, defendant's expert opines that nurse Withrington's conduct was appropriate and within the applicable standard of care. (Mychaliska Decl., Paras. 7a-7i ["If, on the other hand, the perforation was acute and occurred during the repositioning, it would still have been a recognized complication which occurred during appropriate positioning measures, absent substandard (negligent) care."].) Plaintiffs' experts opine that nurse Withrington's conduct fell below the standard of care by only "supporting and securing the catheter" and not firmly pressing the cannula against the patient's neck. (Crowninshield Decl., Paras. 13-14; see also Subramanyan Decl., Paras. 13-17; see also Withrington Depo. 48:3-5 ["My fingers were supporting the catheter in place on the neck, not necessarily pressing, just supporting it in place."].) Thus, whether or not nurse Withrington's hand positioning of the cannula while repositioning plaintiff, Damon, fell below the standard of care and ultimately caused the perforation in Damon's heart is an issue of material fact that cannot be decided on a motion for summary judgment.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR# 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com, NOT Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answer To Deposition Question
Off Calendar Jul-09-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFFs DAMON DAVILA and LINDSAY BOWMAN's Motion To Compel Answer To Deposition Question

Motion to compel GRANTED. The instruction not to answer was inconsistent with CCP 2025.460. As with relevance, "asked and answered" is not a permissible basis for an instruction not to answer. Stewart v. Colonial Western Agency, Inc. (2001) 87 Cal.App.4th 1006, 1014-15. Moreover, because the answer to the earlier similar question was "I do not recall," there is nothing inherently improper for counsel to rephrase a question to see if the different phrasing prompts a recollection. If counsel believed the examination was being conducted in bad faith or was unreasonably oppressive, she should have suspended under CCP 2025.470.

order signed.

Judge Pro Tem: Scott Borrowman, Clerk: S. Le, Not reported. =(302/JPT)
